title: Constitution and Bylaws of the Confederated Tribes of the Umatilla Indian Reservation
issuing_entity: Confederated Tribes of the Umatilla Indian Reservation
covered_parties: Members, the General Council, the Board of Trustees, the Tribal Court, and officers governed by the CTUIR Constitution and Bylaws.
covered_data: Constitutional governance structure, membership, treaty-rights protections, elections, institutional powers, and internal records and reporting duties; not a partner data-sharing instrument.

requirements.none_reason: This constitution establishes governmental structure, powers, rights, elections, and internal duties but states no partner-facing data-sharing or data-handling obligation.

tsdf_mapping.notes: A constitutional governance framework without a partner-facing data rule does not establish TSDF classification, access, sharing, or egress behavior.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction; no Nation-attributed interpretation is confirmed by the Nation. The PDF identifies itself as current text incorporating Amendments I through XVIII, through 2021.

title: Constitution and By-Laws of the Confederated Tribes of the Warm Springs Reservation of Oregon
issuing_entity: Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs Reservation of Oregon
covered_parties: Members, the Tribal Council, officers, and governmental bodies governed by the Warm Springs Constitution and By-Laws.
covered_data: Constitutional objectives, territory, membership, Tribal Council organization and powers, referendum, rights, lands, amendments, and internal officer and records duties; not a partner data-sharing instrument.

requirements.none_reason: This constitution and by-laws establish a governmental framework and internal duties but state no partner-facing data-sharing or data-handling obligation.

tsdf_mapping.notes: The governance framework does not itself define a TSDF-relevant partner access, classification, sharing, or egress rule.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction; no Nation-attributed interpretation is confirmed by the Nation. The official PDF compiles fourteen amendments through the 1992 amendment, but continuing currency should be checked with the Nation before reliance.

title: Constitution and Bylaws of the Shoshone-Paiute Tribes of the Duck Valley Indian Reservation
covered_parties: The Shoshone-Paiute Tribes and persons governed by the constitution identified on the official landing page; the retrieved bytes do not provide enough substantive text to define applicability further.
covered_data: Landing-page title, approval date, preamble heading, and article headings for the constitution and bylaws; the substantive constitutional text is not present in the retrieved bytes.

requirements.none_reason: The retrieved page is a thin constitution landing page with headings and an approval date, not the substantive instrument, so it yields no partner-facing data obligation.

tsdf_mapping.notes: The landing-page bytes contain no substantive data-classification, access, sharing, or egress rule to map.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction. The page identifies an April 20, 1936 approval and an amended membership article but is incomplete; current legal effect and full text require Nation-authorized review.

title: Constitution and Bylaws of the Shoshone-Bannock Tribes of the Fort Hall Reservation
covered_parties: Members, the Fort Hall Business Council, officers, Tribal governmental bodies, and other parties addressed by the Shoshone-Bannock Constitution and Bylaws.
covered_data: Constitutional territory, membership, government, elections, powers, rights, land, referendum, amendments, and internal records and reporting duties; not a partner data-sharing instrument.

requirements.none_reason: The constitution and bylaws establish sovereign government, rights, land, elections, and internal records duties but state no partner-facing data-sharing obligation.

tsdf_mapping.notes: The constitutional governance framework does not itself establish TSDF data classification, partner sharing, or egress controls.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction; no Nation-attributed interpretation is confirmed by the Nation. The official page states approval in 1936 and amendment through August 15, 2013; current effect should be confirmed before legal reliance.

title: Shoshone-Bannock Privacy Policy
covered_parties: Operators and visitors of the Shoshone-Bannock Tribes' public website, with contracted third parties covered only by the research-analysis disclosure described by the policy.
covered_data: Website access telemetry, cookies, web logs, user-submitted personal information, and personal information made available through third-party social-media sites.

claims.claim: When a person visits the Tribal website, the site may store the visitor's network address, visit date and time, referrer, searched file or words, clicked items, browser, and operating system.
claims.conditions: Applies to visits to the public website; the policy says some or all of the listed technical information may be stored.
claims.cite: Information Collected and Stored Automatically, first paragraph

claims.claim: The website describes using automatically collected technical information to measure visits, identify performance problems, develop and analyze the site, and improve usefulness, while not using it to associate search terms or navigation patterns with individual users.
claims.conditions: Applies to the automatically stored visit information described by the policy.
claims.cite: Information Collected and Stored Automatically, second paragraph

claims.claim: The website may provide automatically collected visit information to third-party entities under contract for research analysis.
claims.conditions: The disclosure is described as occasional, limited to contracted third-party entities, and for research-analysis purposes.
claims.cite: Information Collected and Stored Automatically, second paragraph

claims.claim: When a visitor chooses to submit personal information through the website, the website describes collecting and using it only to respond to the request and help provide the requested information.
claims.conditions: Applies only when a visitor voluntarily submits personal information through the website.
claims.cite: If You Send Us Personal Information

claims.claim: Except for authorized law-enforcement investigations, the website describes making no other attempt to identify individual users or their usage habits, using raw data logs for no other purposes, and scheduling those logs for regular destruction under the cited records guidance.
claims.conditions: The non-identification exception is limited to authorized law-enforcement investigations; the destruction statement applies to raw data logs.
claims.cite: Site Security, second paragraph

claims.claim: The website describes neither collecting personally identifiable information through the social-media sites it manages nor using personal information a user makes available to those third-party sites.
claims.conditions: Applies to the Tribe's managed presence on third-party social-media sites.
claims.cite: Social Media Sites

claims.claim: Users must not attempt without authorization to upload or change information on the website, and such attempts may be punishable under the federal laws identified by the policy.
claims.conditions: The prohibition and possible penalties apply to unauthorized upload or information-change attempts on the service.
claims.cite: Site Security, third paragraph

tsdf_mapping.notes: Purpose limitation, contracted disclosure, non-identification, and log destruction resemble controlled handling, but the policy does not classify information into tiers or specify agreement, egress, or sovereign review controls.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction from a public website privacy statement; it is not presented as enacted law, and no Nation-attributed interpretation is confirmed by the Nation.

title: Tribal Code
issuing_entity: Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs Reservation of Oregon

covered_data: Index metadata and links for uploaded Tribal Code chapters, including indications that some chapters are absent or in progress; the linked chapters' substantive text is not part of the retrieved bytes.

requirements.none_reason: The retrieved document is an official code portal listing linked chapters, not the text of an enacted chapter, and states no partner-facing data-governance requirement.

tsdf_mapping.notes: An index of linked legal materials does not itself establish a TSDF-relevant classification, access, sharing, or egress rule.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction; no Nation-attributed claim is confirmed by the Nation. Catalog scope is limited to the retrieved portal bytes and does not infer terms from linked chapters.

title: Northwest Tribal Epidemiology Center Data Governance Handbook, Version 1.1
covered_parties: Northwest Tribal Epidemiology Center and NPAIHB staff, project directors, data stewards and custodians, contractors, consultants, interns, external collaborators, data requesters, member Tribes, and data providers, but only as each handbook provision specifies.
covered_data: Tribal, state, and federal public-health data; record-level PII and PHI; the Northwest Tribal Registry; Tribal Data Hub data; Tribe- and Tribal-area-specific aggregates; analytic files, transfers, reports, publications, media releases, and destruction records.

claims.claim: NPAIHB employees requesting Tribal data for an NPAIHB project must make the request in writing to the Tribal council, state the proposed use and users and how confidentiality will be maintained, include a sample resolution or data-sharing agreement authorizing release, and obtain the NPAIHB Executive Director's approval.
claims.conditions: Applies to employee requests for Tribal data needed for NPAIHB projects; the request contents and executive approval are quoted from the handbook's incorporated NPAIHB policy.
claims.cite: Protection of Tribal Data (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: Records obtained for NPAIHB projects that contain personal or Tribe-specific information must be protected from uses other than those for which they were collected, limited to personnel assigned to the project, and not disclosed without prior written consent from the individual or Tribal government.
claims.conditions: Applies to project records containing personal or Tribe-specific information; consent is attributed to the affected individual or Tribal government as applicable.
claims.cite: Protection of Tribal Data (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: Access to record-level PII and PHI must be limited to the minimum number of people who need it for their job functions, with the appropriate level of access determined by the designated NWTEC leadership.
claims.conditions: Applies to record-level PII and PHI; the handbook separately assigns authorization for the Registry and Data Hub to their project leadership and procedures.
claims.cite: Authorization to Access Data (PDF pp. 10-11)

claims.claim: NWTEC staff, interns, contractors, external collaborators, and other people with access to managed PII or PHI must complete the applicable security, confidentiality, and documentation requirements.
claims.conditions: Asset-specific requirements vary. Some short-term or limited-scope contractors and interns may be exempt from some requirements, but at minimum must complete the NWTEC confidentiality pledge and IT-required security training; specified asset classes state no exceptions.
claims.cite: Training and Documentation Requirements; Table 1 (PDF pp. 11-14)

claims.claim: Contractors, consultants, and interns are expected to follow internal and external policies for protecting data and maintaining confidentiality.
claims.conditions: Applies to contractors, consultants, and interns, including people whose short-term or limited-scope engagement may alter separate training and documentation requirements.
claims.cite: Contractors, Consultants, and Interns (PDF p. 11)

claims.claim: Users accessing NPAIHB network-server data must use an NPAIHB-issued computer, connect on site or through the NPAIHB VPN, authenticate with a unique user ID and a password meeting the stated complexity and rotation rules, and use multi-factor authentication in the stated circumstances.
claims.conditions: Applies to information stored on NPAIHB network servers; multi-factor authentication is required for Office 365, a new device, or a password change.
claims.cite: Requirements for Network Access (PDF pp. 14-15)

claims.claim: Record-level PHI from any source and record-level or aggregated Tribal data must be stored in restricted-access server locations available only to designated people, with folder access approved by the NWTEC Director.
claims.conditions: Applies to NPAIHB server storage of the identified PHI and Tribal data; the handbook notes that IT creates the restricted locations and updates access lists on request.
claims.cite: Storage of PHI and Tribal Data on NPAIHB's servers (PDF p. 15)

claims.claim: An analyst storing a necessary local copy of PHI or Tribal data must first ensure the computer uses the specified validated AES-256 encryption and authenticated login, and the user must securely shred the data after analysis is complete.
claims.conditions: Local storage is not advised and is allowed only where necessary; the encryption and destruction requirements apply before local download and after analysis, respectively.
claims.cite: Storage of PHI and Tribal Data on individual computers (PDF pp. 15-16)

claims.claim: Use of the Northwest Tribal Registry must follow the handbook's layered safeguards: the linkage device remains off public networks during linkages, is secured when not in use, stays with staff during travel, uses secure shredding for confidential files, maintains only encrypted backup storage, and is accessible only to the minimum approved and trained staff.
claims.conditions: Applies specifically to the Registry file, linkage device, and encrypted backup described by the handbook.
claims.cite: Storage and Use of Personally Identifiable Information/NTR files (PDF p. 16)

claims.claim: An appropriate agreement, such as a completed data-sharing agreement, must be in place before a data transfer occurs.
claims.conditions: The handbook states this as the general transfer rule; later sections describe a limited exception for returning certain aggregated state or federal data to a requesting Tribe and distinguish when particular Tribal agreements and addenda are required.
claims.cite: Data Transfers (PDF p. 17)

claims.claim: NWTEC staff should use agency-operated secure portals or secure file-transfer sites for state and federal transfers and should use the identified secure SharePoint process for confidential transfers with Tribes or other partners.
claims.conditions: The first method applies where state or federal providers make their secure services available; SharePoint is described as the preferred method for confidential Tribal and other partner datasets.
claims.cite: Data Transfers (PDF p. 17)

claims.claim: Retention, return, and destruction of data must follow the governing provider agreement; for project-specific Tribal data without such guidance, NWTEC must ask the Tribe at project end for its destruction preference and permanently destroy copies left on electronic transfer platforms after confirming collaborators have the required project files and outputs.
claims.conditions: Active agreements may permit retention of ongoing surveillance data. For project-specific Tribal data, the DSA controls; the direct Tribal follow-up applies only when no destruction or return guidance is available.
claims.cite: When should data be destroyed? (PDF p. 17)

claims.claim: For a known or suspected loss of sensitive information, NWTEC must follow the internal escalation process and notify data owners, DSA contacts, or co-investigators in writing within one week.
claims.conditions: The one-week notice applies unless a signed DSA states otherwise or public-safety personnel or the IRB directs otherwise; additional reporting applies to stolen devices, linkage data, and IRB research.
claims.cite: Protocol for reporting a data breach or loss (PDF p. 18)

claims.claim: Data about an individual Tribe or Tribal service area may be released only with permission from the authorizing official identified in that Tribe's DSA.
claims.conditions: Applies to Tribe- or Tribal-area-specific data and the authorizing official or alternate specified in the Tribe's DSA.
claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)

claims.claim: Under no circumstances should Tribe-specific data be released to an entity outside that Tribe without the Tribe's written permission.
claims.conditions: Applies to entities outside the Tribe; the handbook uses 'should' even with the 'under no circumstances' qualifier.
claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)

claims.claim: Staff must release Tribe-specific data only to people employed by the Tribe who are authorized to receive it.
claims.conditions: Staff must avoid inadvertent release to IHS clinic personnel, contractors, consultants, or unauthorized recipients included in multi-recipient communications unless the Tribe has provided the required permission.
claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)

claims.claim: A release about a sub-state region encompassing multiple Tribes may require written permission from those Tribes or a data-sharing agreement.
claims.conditions: Applies to data for sub-state geographies that encompass multiple Tribes; the handbook does not make permission categorically mandatory for every such region.
claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)

claims.claim: Staff must consult their supervisor or the NWTEC Director to determine the appropriate approach for releasing data about a sub-state region encompassing multiple Tribes.
claims.conditions: Applies to proposed releases about multi-Tribe sub-state regions and does not itself decide whether permission or a DSA is required in a particular case.
claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)

claims.claim: When an outside entity requests Tribe-specific data, NWTEC may respond by having the Tribe request and then release the data, having the outside entity seek the Tribe's written authorization for NWTEC release, or establishing a multiparty data-sharing agreement among the entity, Tribe, and NWTEC.
claims.conditions: The available option depends on staff capacity and the requesting circumstances; the handbook presents these as response options rather than guaranteed access.
claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF pp. 24-25)

claims.claim: A Tribe must have a DSA and Data Hub addendum to access the Northwest Tribal Data Hub, and a DSA and General Data Exchange addendum are required for exchange of Tribally collected data between a Tribe and NWTEC.
claims.conditions: A DSA is preferred but not currently required for data-request technical-assistance responses; aggregated state and federal data may be returned to Tribes without a DSA only when released under the handbook's Tribe-specific release guidance.
claims.cite: Data Sharing Agreements with Tribes (PDF p. 25)

claims.claim: NWTEC reporting should suppress nonzero counts below five and rates or proportions calculated from such counts.
claims.conditions: This is stated as the handbook's general suppression guideline; state and federal systems may impose higher thresholds and additional system-specific restrictions that take precedence.
claims.cite: Small Numbers and Data Suppression Guidance (PDF p. 25)

claims.claim: When presenting data, analysts must ensure suppressed cells cannot be recalculated by aggregating data or applying secondary suppression as necessary.
claims.conditions: Applies to presentations containing suppressed cells; aggregation and secondary suppression are the stated methods for preventing reconstruction.
claims.cite: Small Numbers and Data Suppression Guidance (PDF p. 26)

claims.claim: Analysts must follow the handbook's data-provider-specific release restrictions, including applicable higher suppression thresholds, prohibitions on identifiable or line-level releases, permission requirements for identified institutions, and internal-use-only limitations.
claims.conditions: The exact restriction depends on the named state or federal data system; the table contains system-specific exceptions and does not create a single threshold for all datasets.
claims.cite: Small Numbers and Data Suppression Guidance, provider table (PDF pp. 26-29)

claims.claim: A peer-reviewed publication with an NWTEC staff co-author must be reviewed and approved by the Portland Area IHS IRB before publication.
claims.conditions: Applies to peer-reviewed publications that include NWTEC staff as co-authors.
claims.cite: Review Requirements for Peer-Reviewed Publications and Conference Presentations and other Data Releases (PDF p. 29)

claims.claim: A conference presentation reporting analytic findings should be submitted to the Portland Area IHS IRB before presentation.
claims.conditions: The handbook permits submission after the abstract has been officially accepted and provides a project-protocol route for presentations using data stewarded by the identified NWTEC units.
claims.cite: Review Requirements for Peer-Reviewed Publications and Conference Presentations and other Data Releases (PDF p. 30)

claims.claim: Publications and other products using covered provider data must satisfy the applicable provider-specific review, approval, advance-notice, attribution, and final-copy requirements summarized by the handbook.
claims.conditions: Requirements and deadlines vary by data system. The RHINO review rule exempts urgent Tribal requests supporting immediate public-health action from prerelease program review but still requires copies of developed products as stated in the table.
claims.cite: Review Requirements, provider table (PDF pp. 30-31)

claims.claim: Authors of peer-reviewed manuscripts and conference abstracts should invite qualifying project leadership and staff involved in data acquisition, quality improvement, and preparation to be co-authors, and should acknowledge Northwest Tribes or NPAIHB delegates when possible.
claims.conditions: The co-author invitation remains subject to the handbook's authorship criteria and review requirements; acknowledgement is qualified by 'when possible.'
claims.cite: Authorship and Co-Authorship Guidelines (PDF p. 31)

claims.claim: Media data responses must follow the handbook and NPAIHB media policy; employees must not speak for NPAIHB without express executive approval, and aggregated social-media data releases must follow the handbook and social-media guidance and receive executive approval before release.
claims.conditions: Applies to media inquiries and aggregated data released through social-media channels; inquiries are directed to the institutional executive role.
claims.cite: Release of data to the media and through social media channels (PDF p. 32)

claims.claim: For limited datasets containing record-level health information, access must be least-privilege and time-limited, record-level data must not be re-released to nonstaff or contractors without provider approval, storage and transfer must use the stated encryption controls, and datasets must remain segregated or otherwise distinguishable for return, destruction, and breach assessment.
claims.conditions: These are general appendix requirements for state and federal limited datasets; the applicable DSA may add system-specific requirements.
claims.cite: Appendix A, limited datasets with record-level health information (PDF pp. 36-37)

claims.claim: Aggregated Tribe- or Tribal-area-specific data may be provided only to authorized people within the Tribe or people the Tribe designates, and must not be released at a level that could identify an individual.
claims.conditions: Applies to aggregated data released through products, the Data Hub, or other means and incorporates the handbook's suppression guidelines.
claims.cite: Appendix A, aggregated data (PDF p. 37)

tsdf_mapping.notes: Agreement gates, least-privilege access, restricted storage, secure transfer, destruction, breach notice, Tribal permission, suppression, and publication review resemble negotiated T2 controls and sovereign T3 handling. Provider-specific consent, benefit-sharing, review bodies, purpose limits, incident deadlines, and conditional exceptions are not fully represented by tier assignment.

sensitivity_flags.notes: Cataloged under the owner-approved institution-not-person rule; no individual identity, individual email address, or direct line is present in this record.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction of an intertribal handbook, not a Nation enactment. Correction ev-director-0141 governs the manifest's conservative nation-authored mislabel. Formal adoption or internal enforceability is not established by the retrieved bytes, so legal status remains nonbinding guidance.

title: THRO Overview: Indigenous Data Sovereignty
issuing_entity: National Institutes of Health Tribal Health Research Office
covered_parties: NIH-funded researchers and studies, Tribal Nations and communities, individual participants, Tribal review bodies, and data stewards discussed in the presentation's proposed Indigenous Data Sovereignty policy takeaways; the slides do not establish a final NIH policy.
covered_data: Data and samples collected from or about Tribal populations; Tribal attributions and inferences; human-subject, biological, genetic, genomic, environmental, cultural, and Indigenous Knowledge materials; secondary analyses, publications, transfer, reuse, and disposal.

claims.claim: The presentation's policy-development takeaways state that a Tribe holds authority over data collected on Tribal lands or data from which inferences or attributions to that Tribe can be made, and that ownership and authority over Tribal data rest with the Tribe.
claims.conditions: Presented as a policy-development takeaway rather than an adopted NIH rule.
claims.cite: IDS Policy Development Takeaways (PDF p. 9)

claims.claim: Under the presentation's proposed approach, data management, sharing, access, reuse, transfer, and disposal must receive Tribal approval before data collection.
claims.conditions: Applies to Tribal data within the presentation's IDS policy-development scope; the slides are draft policy material.
claims.cite: IDS Policy Development Takeaways (PDF p. 9)

claims.claim: Future transfer, de-identification, and return plans must be established, and open or unrestricted access must not be the default; a Tribe must affirmatively opt in with clear plans and approvals.
claims.conditions: Presented as a policy-development takeaway for covered Tribal data and future use, not as a final NIH policy.
claims.cite: IDS Policy Development Takeaways (PDF p. 9)

claims.claim: Individual and collective benefits, including dissemination, publication strategies, and benefit sharing, should be stated in consent materials and data plans in plain language.
claims.conditions: The presentation addresses both the Tribe and individual participants and identifies possible agreements as a vehicle for mutual understanding.
claims.cite: IDS Policy Development Takeaways (PDF p. 9)

claims.claim: Research planners must clarify with the Tribe in writing who owns or co-owns the data, who will steward it, how and where it will be stewarded, and what cultural protections apply to the data and participating communities.
claims.conditions: The slide gives this as an imperative within draft policy-development takeaways and before collection planning is finalized.
claims.cite: IDS Policy Development Takeaways (PDF p. 9)

claims.claim: The presentation states that a Tribe has the right to designate the Tribal or non-Tribal IRB or other review body and the single- or multiple-IRB approval process, and that final approval to proceed remains with the Tribe, including any retained secondary review.
claims.conditions: The process and any retained secondary review are to be determined before data collection; Tribal assent alone is not treated as consent.
claims.cite: Tribal review and approval takeaways (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: A process for obtaining formal Tribal review and approval should be established before data collection through the Tribal council or another review body designated by the Tribe.
claims.conditions: The Tribe determines the appropriate approval body; examples in the presentation are illustrative rather than exhaustive.
claims.cite: Tribal review and approval takeaways (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: When relevant, cultural-stewardship protocols for living and nonliving data should be identified before data collection.
claims.conditions: Applies where the data or samples implicate cultural stewardship; the presentation proposes treating covered data and samples with protections comparable to human-subject protections.
claims.cite: Tribal review and approval takeaways (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: A Tribe must follow an individual participant's consent concerning data disposal and must not override that consent.
claims.conditions: Applies where individual consent specifies disposal; the slide states that Tribal authority does not override the individual's disposal choice.
claims.cite: Individual rights and disposal takeaways (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: Legacy and dynamic re-consent plans must receive Tribal approval before data collection, and consent planning for data associated with deceased people must identify disposition, transfer, disposal, and who will decide.
claims.conditions: The slide addresses longitudinal and deceased-person data; it recommends identifying a re-consent timeframe if disposal is not planned.
claims.cite: Individual rights and disposal takeaways (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: Tribal consent and re-consent must be obtained for analyses or secondary analyses that may identify a Tribe or create impacts, inferences, or implications for that Tribe.
claims.conditions: Triggered by Tribal identification, inference, implication, or impact rather than by every off-reservation analysis.
claims.cite: Biological samples and Tribal consent takeaways (PDF p. 11)

claims.claim: The presentation recommends ending irreversible and timeless consent for biological, genetic, genomic, and derived materials.
claims.conditions: Presented as a proposed IDS practice rather than an adopted prohibition.
claims.cite: Biological samples and Tribal consent takeaways (PDF p. 11)

claims.claim: Clear biological-sample consent options and consent or re-consent plans must be identified at the outset.
claims.conditions: Applies to the sample categories covered by the slide and is presented as a policy-development need.
claims.cite: Biological samples and Tribal consent takeaways (PDF p. 11)

claims.claim: A study may proceed without Tribal approval when it makes no inference to a Tribal community, is not collected on Tribal land or at an IHS urban facility, and involves an off-reservation participant.
claims.conditions: Every stated condition must be satisfied; the slide separately raises overlap with nearby Tribal lands as requiring additional caution.
claims.cite: Points of Tension: urban/off-reservation sample (PDF p. 12)

claims.claim: For an urban sample near or overlapping Tribal lands, consultation with nearby Tribes and an agreement preventing Tribe-specific analysis without Tribal approval are recommended as best practice.
claims.conditions: The recommendation supplements the page's off-reservation criteria and applies to the stated geographic-overlap scenario.
claims.cite: Points of Tension: urban/off-reservation sample (PDF p. 12)

claims.claim: Planned or secondary analyses that will make inferences to a specific Tribe or Tribal community must obtain Tribal consent and formal approval under that Tribe's laws or protocols before conducting the study or analysis or publishing or otherwise disseminating findings.
claims.conditions: Triggered where Tribe-specific inference can affect the social, cultural, economic, or general welfare of the identified Nation, village, or community.
claims.cite: Tribe-specific inference rule (PDF p. 13)

claims.claim: The proposed approach describes honoring a Tribe's request to repatriate samples or transfer them to a designated repository unless doing so conflicts with the original individual's consent.
claims.conditions: The slide gives individual consent to destruction as the example of a controlling conflict.
claims.cite: Points of Consideration: sample repatriation (PDF p. 22)

tsdf_mapping.notes: Tribal approval before collection and reuse, opt-in access, restricted secondary analysis, and repatriation resemble negotiated T2 and sovereign T3 controls. Collective consent, Tribal IRB designation, benefit-sharing, cultural guardianship, re-consent, inference-based scope, and individual-versus-collective disposal rules are not fully represented by TSDF tiers.

sensitivity_flags.notes: Cataloged under the owner-approved institution-not-person rule; no individual identity or individual email address is present in this record.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction of a presentation describing a policy-development process, proposed takeaways, and open questions. It is not represented as a final NIH Indigenous Data Sovereignty policy. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0183.

title: Tribal Data Sovereignty and the Critical Role of Data in Public Health Governance
issuing_entity: American Indian Health Commission for Washington State
covered_parties: Tribal Nations, state and federal public-health jurisdictions, and parties considering the Washington health data-sharing agreement provisions excerpted by the presentation; the slides do not themselves execute or enact those provisions.
covered_data: Tribal and AI/AN public-health data, including data held in state, federal, and other datasets, aggregated Tribal data, COVID-19 surveillance and immunization information, and other public-health systems addressed by the model provisions.

claims.claim: The presentation states as a core principle that only a Tribe has sovereign authority to determine how its data may or may not be used.
claims.conditions: Presented as a nonbinding data-sovereignty principle rather than as an enacted provision.
claims.cite: Principle #1 (PDF p. 10)

claims.claim: A jurisdiction reporting on or about American Indian or Alaska Native peoples should meaningfully partner and consult with Tribal leaders on analysis and interpretation of the data.
claims.conditions: Applies when a jurisdiction reports on or about the identified peoples; the slide attributes this as a core principle.
claims.cite: Principle #2 (PDF p. 11)

claims.claim: The presentation states that Tribes retain an ownership interest in Tribal data when the data are held in state, federal, or other datasets and when the data are aggregated with other data.
claims.conditions: Presented as a nonbinding core principle; the slide does not reproduce a governing ownership instrument for every dataset.
claims.cite: Principle #3 (PDF p. 12)

claims.claim: The presentation states that Tribes must have equitable access to data needed to perform their governmental duties.
claims.conditions: Presented as a core sovereignty principle rather than a self-executing access grant to any named system.
claims.cite: Principle #4 (PDF p. 13)

claims.claim: The presentation describes a sovereignty-aligned Tribal data-sharing agreement as including government-to-government Tribal input on state and federal data use, state protection of Tribal and AI/AN data, recognition of Tribal ownership, and more equitable Tribal access for governmental duties.
claims.conditions: This is guidance on key agreement provisions; the slide does not state that every agreement already contains them.
claims.cite: Tribal Data Sharing Agreements: Key Provisions (PDF p. 17)

claims.claim: In the agreement provision excerpted by the presentation, a Tribal Data Sovereignty Committee must not substitute for the state health department's separate statutory Tribal-consultation duty.
claims.conditions: This claim is limited to the quoted committee provision; the presentation is not itself the executed agreement or the consultation statute.
claims.cite: Provision #1, Appendix E § 1.1 excerpt (PDF p. 18)

claims.claim: The presentation's sample ownership clause describes joint Tribal and state health-department ownership of data about the Tribe, its citizens, and people within its jurisdiction.
claims.conditions: The clause is explicitly template language for a Tribal agreement and remains subject to law and all restrictions and limitations in the separately executed agreement.
claims.cite: Provision #3, Ownership of Data excerpt (PDF p. 20)

claims.claim: Under the access clause excerpted by the presentation, a Tribe or urban Indian health organization may request access to additional state health datasets.
claims.conditions: The request is permissive and does not guarantee access to any additional dataset.
claims.cite: Provision #4, Access to DOH Datasets/Databases excerpt (PDF p. 21)

claims.claim: Any additional dataset access that the state provides under the excerpted clause must be added as an exhibit to the agreement and executed by both parties.
claims.conditions: Triggered only when the state provides access to an additional dataset under the quoted agreement clause; the presentation itself is not the executed exhibit.
claims.cite: Provision #4, Access to DOH Datasets/Databases excerpt (PDF p. 21)

tsdf_mapping.notes: Agreement-based access, Tribal input, and controlled state-held data resemble negotiated T2 and sovereign T3 controls. Tribal ownership interests in external and aggregated datasets, statutory consultation, equitable government access, and joint-ownership clauses are not represented by tier assignment alone.

sensitivity_flags.notes: Cataloged under the owner-approved institution-not-person rule; no presenter identity, individual email address, or personal-domain mailbox is present in this record.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction of a nonbinding presentation. The Bureau of Justice Assistance hosts the file; the presentation identifies the American Indian Health Commission for Washington State as the presenting organization. Quoted agreement clauses are not represented as independently executed by the slides. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0184.

title: Policy Brief: Data Governance for Native Nation Rebuilding
covered_parties: Native Nations and non-Tribal entities discussed by the publication landing page; the retrieved page does not state an operative duty for either group.
covered_data: Indigenous and Tribal data governance, Native nation rebuilding, and the implications of Indigenous data sovereignty for non-Tribal entities that govern Tribal data, as summarized by the landing page.

requirements.none_reason: The retrieved landing page provides only a descriptive abstract and citation for the policy brief; it states no partner-facing data obligation in the captured bytes.

tsdf_mapping.notes: A descriptive abstract without the brief's substantive recommendations or requirements cannot support a TSDF mapping.

sensitivity_flags.notes: Cataloged under the owner-approved institution-not-person rule; no individual identity or individual email address is present in this record.

notes: Machine extraction is limited to the 2017 publication landing page and does not infer claims from the linked or referenced brief. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0185.

title: 78 IAM 2: Data Governance
covered_parties: All programs and offices under Bureau of Indian Affairs authority, including BIA leadership, deputy bureau directors, the associate chief data officer, data stewards, data asset custodians and producers, system owners, and employees, only within each role's assigned responsibilities.
covered_data: All BIA data assets collected, created, or acquired; metadata, information-system schemas and dictionaries, public and nonpublic data, controlled and personally identifiable information, geospatial data, open-government data assets, and data supplied to AI systems.

claims.claim: All BIA programs and offices must comply with applicable data regulations, requirements, and standards and promote sustainable data-management practices supporting the BIA mission.
claims.conditions: Applies throughout the BIA under the policy's stated scope; particular duties remain assigned by role in § 1.5.

claims.claim: The BIA Director must provide resources and organizational support for the Data Governance Program, coordinate policy and guidance with stakeholders, delegate data-management authority, make program objectives and policies clear, and ensure employees are aware of and trained on data policies and guidance.
claims.conditions: Applies to the institutional BIA Director role as Senior Accountable Official for the program.

claims.claim: The identified deputy bureau directors must oversee and periodically review data governance in their areas, provide policy and technical assistance, and assign data stewards and data asset custodians.
claims.conditions: Applies within each deputy director's services, functions, offices, or regions.

claims.claim: The BIA associate chief data officer must implement applicable data directives, policies, and guidelines; maintain data-asset inventories; monitor internal controls; protect data confidentiality, integrity, and availability; and support openness, accuracy, and completeness.
claims.conditions: Applies to data assets collected, created, or acquired under applicable statute, regulation, and departmental policy, with support from the other roles identified in the directive.

claims.claim: A BIA data steward must manage assigned data assets, ensure compliance with departmental directives and data-category, metadata, and quality-control requirements, ensure custodial documentation and maintenance, and complete required data-governance training.
claims.conditions: Applies to data assets within the steward's assigned area of responsibility.

claims.claim: A BIA data asset custodian must manage assigned assets for storage, integrity, and security, make public assets available in a compliant machine-readable format, support implementation of asset requirements, and complete required training.
claims.conditions: Applies within the custodian's technical-management area; public-availability duty is limited to public data assets and departmental standards.

claims.claim: A BIA data asset producer must create or acquire data digitally, create and manage metadata under departmental requirements, and implement quality control for BIA data management.
claims.conditions: Applies to the producer role when it creates, collects, acquires, or distributes data.

claims.claim: A BIA system owner must document every data asset within the system, keep the associate chief data officer, steward, and custodian informed of those assets, and maintain system schemas and data dictionaries.
claims.conditions: Applies to information systems for which the role procures, develops, integrates, modifies, operates, maintains, or disposes.

claims.claim: BIA employees must not disclose nonpublic data, including through a prompt, to a generative-AI system unless the Department has authorized that system for use.
claims.conditions: The prohibition applies only to nonpublic data and lasts unless and until the Department authorizes the specific generative-AI system.

claims.claim: All existing Departmental policies must continue to be followed when BIA uses or procures AI technologies.
claims.conditions: Applies to AI use and procurement within the Department; the directive does not replace more specific policies.

claims.claim: BIA must implement internal controls ensuring that metadata records are created and managed under Department Data Governance Board directives and federal regulations.
claims.conditions: Applies to metadata records governed by the continuing standards in § 1.7.

claims.claim: BIA personnel must follow the Department's open-data inventory policy and make covered data assets open-government data assets by default unless law or regulation restricts doing so.
claims.conditions: Applies to assets collected, created, or acquired after enactment of the OPEN Government Data Act; the open default is expressly limited by legal or regulatory restrictions.

claims.claim: A person must complete the directive's specified minimum courses or authorized equivalents before serving as a BIA data steward or data asset custodian.
claims.conditions: Applies before service in either identified role; authorized equivalent training satisfies the requirement.

claims.claim: After completing the designated courses, data stewards and data asset custodians should notify the BIA associate chief data officer for inclusion in the trained-workforce database.
claims.conditions: Applies as the designated courses are completed and is stated as a recommendation rather than a prerequisite.
claims.cite: § 1.8, final paragraph (PDF p. 8)

tsdf_mapping.notes: The directive's nonpublic-data and role-based controls partially resemble restricted handling, but its statutory open-data default applies unless law or regulation restricts release. TSDF instead treats unclassified data as T3 and can preserve sovereign restrictions not expressed solely as federal law or regulation; the directive contains no TSDF-tier or T3-egress guarantee.

sensitivity_flags.notes: Cataloged under the owner-approved institution-not-person rule; approval is attributed only to the BIA Director role, with no individual identity or individual contact detail present.

notes: Machine extraction treats the issued Indian Affairs Manual chapter as current internal agency policy effective May 13, 2024. The BIA Director approved the directive. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0186.

title: Research Request Form
covered_parties: People making research requests to the Swinomish Tribal Archive; the page addresses requesters and states an institutional submission preference.
covered_data: Research-request information submitted to seek access or service involving Tribal Archive materials, including materials the Archive describes as sensitive.

claims.claim: The Swinomish Tribal Archive requests that all research requests be submitted through its online research-request form.
claims.conditions: The page frames the form as a request, not a mandatory legal prerequisite, and ties it to protecting sensitive materials and providing efficient service.
claims.cite: Research Request Form page, opening paragraph

tsdf_mapping.notes: A structured request channel for sensitive archive materials resembles a negotiated-access gate, but the page does not state approval criteria, classification tiers, authorized uses, or egress rules.

sensitivity_flags.notes: Cataloged under the owner-approved institution-not-person rule; the institutional Archives and Records Manager role is used without any individual identity or direct line.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction; no Nation-attributed claim is confirmed by the Nation. Catalog scope is the landing page, not terms that may appear in the separately linked form. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0187.

title: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP) Agreement
issuing_entity: Kalispel Tribe of Indians and Washington State Department of Commerce
covered_parties: Only the Kalispel Tribe of Indians and the Washington State Department of Commerce, as the two parties to the executed agreement.
covered_data: Household-member names and Social Security numbers, assistance amounts for people moving into or out of Tribal service territory, and applicant-household referrals, only for preventing duplicate LIHEAP payments when necessary.

claims.claim: The Kalispel Tribe and the Washington State Department of Commerce must take necessary steps and identify procedures to prevent duplicate LIHEAP payments to eligible households across their separate funding plans and service areas.
claims.conditions: Applies to the two agreement parties and the prevention of duplicate payments under the LIHEAP agreement.
claims.cite: § 5, Data sovereignty, data sharing and non-duplication of payments (PDF p. 2)

claims.claim: When necessary to prevent duplicate LIHEAP payments, the agreement limits the identified data-sharing procedure to household-member names and Social Security numbers and to assistance amounts for people moving into or out of Tribal service territory.
claims.conditions: The listed information is covered only when necessary for the two parties' duplicate-payment prevention; the agreement does not authorize broader data use in the retrieved clause.
claims.cite: § 5.A-B (PDF p. 2)

claims.claim: The Kalispel Tribe and the Washington State Department of Commerce must make applicant-household referrals when appropriate.
claims.conditions: Applies only when referral is appropriate under the LIHEAP coordination described in § 5.
claims.cite: § 5, final sentence (PDF p. 2)

tsdf_mapping.notes: The executed agreement limits data categories, parties, and purpose and conditions sharing on necessity, which resembles negotiated T2 handling of sensitive data. It does not state classification tiers, storage, retention, redisclosure, audit, or T3 egress rules.

sensitivity_flags.notes: Cataloged under the owner-approved institution-not-person rule; only the two party organizations are named, with no individual identity, individual contact detail, or direct line present.

notes: DRAFT machine extraction of an executed intergovernmental agreement; covered parties are not broadened beyond the two agreement parties. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0188.
